results.text:
I just found out this is very widespread among people looking for jobs in crypto, people I know reported multiple cases too. 

Here are some signs to look for:

- stranger messages you on Linkedin with a great looking job offer
- their current position doesn't add up with what they are recruiting for (or it has just changed)
- they are trying to move communication to e-mail, telegram, etc
- they are trying to create a sense of urgency
- they are trying to get you to download and run something on
results.job_title: - Their Current Doesn't Add Up What They Are Recruiting

results.text:
#AI is replacing roles everywhere. Web3 hiring is down 80%+. The market is shaking people out. 

Most agencies are quietly letting people go.
We’re not.

The standard move is to cut the team and protect margins.

Our move is to protect the people.

Anyone who stayed up till midnight, worked weekends, and believed in this before there was even something to believe in, you don’t cut those people when things get tough.

You back them. You protect them.

Who else is holding? 👇

results.text:
🚨3/5

For candidates: Focus on demonstrable skills.
Update your portfolio with open-source contributions, deployed dApps, or past DeFi roles. In 2026, “proof of work” literally beats pedigree in Web3 hiring.

Saying you built something on-chain carries more weight than a shiny degree.

results.text:
There's a good chance we never see Abstract launch a token. It has been nothing short of a failure. And that's not to blame @LucaNetz. There's just no interest in another L2. 

One mistake I do continue to see is teams hiring the wrong people to push their product. In crypto it's tough to find actual good hires. Most people who are successful and have made it in crypto aren't looking for a job. So they are basically stuck hiring the ones that lack the necessary skills to succeed in the space.
